threats.title: VoteWeighting.removeNominee Corrupts pointsSum Slope, Inflating All Remaining Nominees' Relative Weights
threats.hypothesis_description: When the owner calls removeNominee(), the function correctly zeros the removed nominee's bias at nextTime and adjusts pointsSum[nextTime].bias. However, it does NOT zero pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].slope or adjust pointsSum[nextTime].slope. The removed nominee's slope contribution remains in the global sum slope, causing pointsSum.bias to decay faster than it should in subsequent _getSum() fill-forward iterations. This results in a systematically underestimated totalSum in nomineeRelativeWeight(), inflating the relative weight of ALL remaining nominees and over-distributing OLAS staking incentives from inflation.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct logic error in a core accounting function. The global weight sum slope is permanently corrupted each time a nominee is removed, compounding over multiple removals. Since nomineeRelativeWeight determines how staking inflation OLAS is distributed across all chains, this affects the entire protocol's emission distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].bias = 0 but .slope is NOT zeroed; pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum but .slope is NOT adjusted to remove the removed nominee's slope contribution

preconditions: At least one nominee has been added and has non-zero voting weight
Owner calls removeNominee for that nominee
Other nominees exist with active voting weight

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: removeNominee sets bias to 0 but leaves slope intact in both pointsWeight and pointsSum
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Triggered by normal owner operation of removing deprecated nominees
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent corruption from moment of removal until all voters revoke their power from the removed nominee

operational_questions: How much slope remains in pointsSum after removal of a nominee with significant voting weight?
What is the cumulative bias underestimation over multiple weeks with corrupted slope?
Does the excess OLAS distribution from inflated relative weights exceed meaningful thresholds?
validation_requirements: Deploy VoteWeighting with multiple nominees, vote for them, remove one, verify pointsSum.slope still includes removed nominee's contribution
Calculate expected vs actual nomineeRelativeWeight for remaining nominees over subsequent weeks
Verify OLAS distribution amounts in Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives are inflated

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker votes for their own nominee staking target with veOLAS
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner removes a different nominee (normal admin operation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: pointsSum slope is now corrupted, inflating attacker's relative weight
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker claims staking incentives via Dispenser, receiving more OLAS than their proportional share
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: No coordination needed; attacker passively benefits from any nominee removal

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Entire emission weight system distorted per removal event
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All staking incentive distributions become incorrectly proportioned
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No in-protocol mechanism to repair corrupted pointsSum slope; requires contract upgrade
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All nomineeRelativeWeight calculations, all staking incentive claims across all chains

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Any amount of veOLAS sufficient to vote for a nominee
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None - attacker benefits passively from normal admin operations
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of gauge weight mechanics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after a nominee is removed

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Compare pointsSum.slope before and after removeNominee; check if slope decreases appropriately
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Remaining nominees' relative weights summing to more than expected
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Increasing totalSum underestimation over time after removals

threats.title: Unrevoked Votes on Removed Nominees Leave Residual changesSum Entries That Further Distort Global Weight Decay
threats.hypothesis_description: When a nominee is removed via VoteWeighting.removeNominee(), the changesWeight and changesSum entries scheduled at voters' lock expiration times are NOT cleaned up. These entries remain and will be processed by _getSum() at the corresponding future timestamps, further reducing pointsSum.slope at times that no longer correspond to any active nominee's decay schedule. Voters must individually call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower() to clean up, but there is no economic incentive to do so (only gas cost). This creates an uncontrolled degradation of the weight sum over time.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even after the immediate slope corruption from OPS-H001, the scheduled changesSum entries create ongoing distortion. Each voter's lock expiration triggers an additional slope reduction in pointsSum that shouldn't exist for removed nominees. The cumulative effect depends on how many voters fail to revoke.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeNominee does not iterate over voters to clean changesSum/changesWeight entries; relies on individual revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower calls which have no incentive mechanism

preconditions: Multiple voters have voted for a nominee that gets removed
Voters do not call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower after removal
Voters' veOLAS locks expire at future timestamps

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: changesSum entries for removed nominee's voters remain active and will execute at lock expiration
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Triggered by owner removing nominee; exacerbated by voter inaction
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From removal until all voters either revoke or their locks expire

operational_questions: What percentage of voters typically call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower?
How does the changesSum residual affect pointsSum.slope at each voter lock expiration?
Can pointsSum.slope become 0 prematurely, causing bias to stop decaying?
validation_requirements: Simulate nominee removal with multiple voters, track changesSum over time
Verify that changesSum[voterLockEnd] still contains the voter's slope contribution post-removal
Measure cumulative totalSum distortion over multiple weeks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Multiple users vote for nominee X
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users do not revoke their votes (no incentive to spend gas)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: changesSum entries at lock expiration times fire, further distorting global slope
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - passive; exploiter benefits from voter inaction

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Global weight sum accounting progressively degrades
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Emission distribution becomes increasingly skewed over time
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires each voter to manually revoke, or a contract upgrade to batch-clean

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None for exploitation; attacker is passive beneficiary
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Progressive over weeks/months following nominee removal

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Check changesSum at future timestamps for non-zero entries corresponding to removed nominees
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: pointsSum.slope decreasing at times unrelated to active nominee schedule
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: voteUserSlopes entries for removed nominees still active

threats.title: EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit Hard Reverts Entire Batch If Any Single Target Has Zero Emissions
threats.hypothesis_description: In EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit(), if any single staking target in the batch returns a zero limitAmount from verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(), the function hard reverts with TargetEmissionsZero. This differs from L2 DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData which gracefully withholds invalid amounts and continues. Since Dispenser.claimStakingIncentivesBatch calls sendMessageBatch for all targets on a chain atomically, one invalid L1 target blocks ALL L1 staking targets in the batch. A deployer can weaponize this by creating an instance, getting it voted for as a nominee, then disabling it via setInstanceStatus.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric error handling between L1 (hard revert) and L2 (soft withhold) creates a DoS vector unique to L1 staking targets. Since nominee addition is permissionless, an attacker can add their own staking contract, attract votes, then disable it to block batch claims.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (limitAmount == 0) { revert TargetEmissionsZero(target); } — hard revert instead of graceful handling; compare with DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData which uses continue

preconditions: At least one staking target on L1 chain becomes invalid (disabled or fails verification)
A batch claim includes both the invalid target and valid targets on L1 chain
Attacker or deployer has disabled the staking instance via setInstanceStatus

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: EthereumDepositProcessor reverts entire batch on single failure vs L2 dispensers' graceful handling
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Attacker can deploy and disable their own instance; no admin involvement needed
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: As long as the invalid target remains in the batch being claimed

operational_questions: Can users identify which target is invalid and exclude it from their batch claim?
What is the gas/UX cost of switching from batch to individual claims?
Can a malicious deployer keep toggling isEnabled to intermittently DoS claims?
validation_requirements: Deploy staking instance, get it added as nominee, disable it, verify batch claim reverts
Confirm individual claimStakingIncentives calls for valid targets still work
Verify the deployer toggle attack is possible without time restrictions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Add instance as nominee via addNomineeEVM (permissionless)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Vote for nominee with minimal veOLAS to get it included in claims
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single attacker with small veOLAS allocation

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: All L1 batch staking claims blocked
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users must identify invalid target and use individual claims at higher gas cost
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Deployer must re-enable or nominee must be removed by owner

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Disable instance before batch claims are processed

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Batch claim failures on L1 chain
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Newly created instances being voted for then quickly disabled

threats.title: DefaultTargetDispenserL2.redeem Deposits OLAS to Staking Targets Without Re-Verifying Current Validity
threats.hypothesis_description: The redeem() function in DefaultTargetDispenserL2 allows anyone to fulfill queued staking deposits by matching a queueHash (target, amount, batchNonce). However, it only checks that the hash was queued; it does NOT re-verify the staking target against stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(). A target that was valid when _processData queued the deposit but was subsequently disabled (or had its emissions capped lower) can still receive the full queued OLAS amount through redeem.
threats.investigation_rationale: The verification happens only at queue time in _processData (low-level call). Between queuing and redemption, the staking target's validity may change. This creates a window where OLAS can be sent to targets that should no longer receive emissions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: redeem() checks only stakingQueueingNonces[queueHash] == true; no call to stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount(target) before approve+deposit

preconditions: A staking target was valid when _processData ran but was subsequently disabled
OLAS was queued for that target (insufficient balance or paused at processing time)
Contract is unpaused and has sufficient OLAS balance for redemption

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: redeem() bypasses all verification, directly approves and deposits to any queued target
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Anyone can call redeem for any queued hash
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between queue time and redeem time; target validity can change

operational_questions: How often are deposits queued vs immediately fulfilled?
Can a target deployer disable an instance between queue and redeem to extract OLAS?
Does the staking contract deposit function have any protections against disabled instances?
validation_requirements: Queue a deposit for a valid target, then disable the target, then call redeem
Verify OLAS is transferred to the disabled target without reverting
Check if the staking contract's deposit function has its own validity check

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy staking instance, get it voted for and included in claims
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for a batch where dispenser is paused or has insufficient balance (deposits get queued)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Disable the staking instance via setInstanceStatus
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call redeem() with the queued hash to send OLAS to the now-invalid target
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Deployer controls both instance status and can trigger redeem

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: OLAS sent to targets that no longer meet emissions criteria
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Emissions sent to contracts that may not distribute them properly
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: OLAS locked in the staking target; recovery depends on target contract logic

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires queued deposit to exist
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must act between queuing and another party's redemption

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Queued deposits for recently disabled instances
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Redemptions to instances that fail current verification
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Instance status changes shortly after deposits are queued

threats.title: Dispenser.retain() Callable During Staking Incentive Pause, Advancing Retainer Epoch Counter and Modifying Staking Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: The retain() function in Dispenser.sol lacks any pause state check, unlike claimStakingIncentives and claimStakingIncentivesBatch which both verify paused state and treasury paused state. This allows anyone to call retain() during a StakingIncentivesPaused or AllPaused state, advancing mapLastClaimedStakingEpochs[retainerHash] and calling ITokenomics.refundFromStaking() which modifies the current epoch's staking incentive accounting. During a security pause meant to freeze all staking-related operations, retain() remains operational and can alter staking inflation state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pause mechanism is specifically designed to halt staking incentive flows during emergencies. retain() is functionally part of the staking incentive system (it returns unused incentives to inflation), so its exclusion from pause checks appears to be an oversight.

proof_of_signal.pattern: retain() has reentrancy guard but NO pause check; contrast with claimStakingIncentives lines 490-493 which check paused == StakingIncentivesPaused || paused == AllPaused || ITreasury(treasury).paused() == 2

preconditions: Dispenser is in StakingIncentivesPaused or AllPaused state
Retainer has unclaimed epochs
Retainer has non-zero voting weight

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: retain() operates on staking incentive data without pause protection
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Anyone can call retain() at any time regardless of pause state
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any staking incentive pause period

operational_questions: Does calling retain() during pause cause any state inconsistency with post-unpause operations?
Can refundFromStaking during pause affect epoch settlement calculations?
Is the advancing of retainerHash epoch counter recoverable?
validation_requirements: Set pause state to StakingIncentivesPaused, call retain(), verify it succeeds
Verify claimStakingIncentives reverts in the same pause state
Check if refundFromStaking changes affect checkpoint() calculations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for owner to pause staking incentives (e.g., during security incident)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call retain() to advance retainer's claimed epochs and refund to inflation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: This modifies staking incentive accounting during what should be a frozen state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: After unpause, the staking incentive distribution reflects the mid-pause retain() effects

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Pause protection for staking incentive accounting is incomplete
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Staking inflation state modified during emergency pause
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - effects are limited to inflation pool refunds

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Staking inflation changes during emergency pause
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Opportunistic retain() calls timed with pause events

threats.title: Tokenomics.refundFromStaking Error Message References Wrong Manager Address (depository Instead of dispenser)
threats.hypothesis_description: In Tokenomics.refundFromStaking(), the access control check verifies that msg.sender equals the dispenser address, but the error message incorrectly reports the depository address as the expected manager: 'revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository)'. This is a clear code defect where the error parameter should be 'dispenser' instead of 'depository'. While the access control logic itself is correct, the misleading error information could cause debugging confusion and delay incident response.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a factual code error that represents a copy-paste or variable substitution mistake. While it does not compromise access control security, it provides incorrect diagnostic information that could mislead developers and operators during troubleshooting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (dispenser != msg.sender) { revert ManagerOnly(msg.sender, depository); } — condition checks 'dispenser' but error reports 'depository'

preconditions: Any address other than dispenser calls refundFromStaking

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Error message variable does not match the access control check variable
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: N/A - anyone triggering the revert sees the wrong address

operational_questions: Could misleading error info delay incident response in production?
Are there other similar variable mismatches in access control error messages?
validation_requirements: Call refundFromStaking from a non-dispenser address, verify error shows depository instead of dispenser

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A - not an exploitable attack, but a confirmed code defect

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None - access control logic is correct

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted refundFromStaking transactions with incorrect manager in error

threats.title: Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives Reverts Instead of Returning Zero When No Incentives Are Available
threats.hypothesis_description: In Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives(), when both reward and topUp are zero, the success variable remains false (it is only set inside the if (reward + topUp > 0) block). The function then reverts with ClaimIncentivesFailed. This means any caller with zero accumulated incentives receives a confusing revert instead of a graceful zero return. This breaks composability with external contracts that call claimOwnerIncentives expecting it not to revert, and creates poor UX for users checking their incentive status.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function should either return (0, 0) gracefully or skip the treasury call and set success = true when there's nothing to claim. The current behavior makes it impossible to safely call this function from a batched transaction or external contract without pre-checking incentive balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: success variable initialized as false, only set to true inside if ((reward + topUp) > 0) block; if both are 0, reaches if (!success) { revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(msg.sender, 0, 0); }

preconditions: Caller has no accumulated rewards or top-ups for the provided unit types and IDs
Function is not paused

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Function always reverts on zero incentives instead of returning gracefully
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: N/A - affects any user with no incentives

operational_questions: Are there external contracts or batched transactions that depend on claimOwnerIncentives not reverting?
Can an attacker exploit this to DoS a multi-call transaction by including a zero-incentive claim?
Should the function be updated to return (0, 0) gracefully?
validation_requirements: Call claimOwnerIncentives with unit IDs that have zero accumulated incentives
Verify the function reverts with ClaimIncentivesFailed instead of returning (0, 0)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a contract or multi-call pattern that includes claimOwnerIncentives
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If the call is for units with no incentives, the entire multicall reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: This blocks other operations in the same transaction batch

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - callers must pre-check incentives via getOwnerIncentives

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time when incentives are zero

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed claimOwnerIncentives transactions with zero reward and topUp
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected reverts in batched claim operations

threats.title: Silent uint96 Truncation in Tokenomics._finalizeIncentivesForUnitId Can Cause Permanent Reward/TopUp Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: In _finalizeIncentivesForUnitId(), the accumulated totalIncentives for both reward and topUp are cast to uint96 without overflow checking: 'mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].reward = uint96(totalIncentives)'. If a component/agent accumulates enough reward across multiple epochs without claiming (totalIncentives exceeds ~7.9e28), the cast silently truncates the value, permanently destroying the excess reward. The reward calculation adds the existing uint96 reward to (pendingRelativeReward * rewardUnitFraction / 100), which under high donation volumes could exceed uint96 max.
threats.investigation_rationale: The totalIncentives variable is computed as a uint256 but stored as uint96 without bounds checking. While reaching uint96 max requires significant accumulation, the protocol is designed to run for decades with growing usage. A single high-value component used across many services could accumulate rewards that approach this limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalIncentives = mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].reward + totalIncentives / 100; mapUnitIncentives[unitType][unitId].reward = uint96(totalIncentives); — no overflow check before uint96 cast

preconditions: A unit (component/agent) accumulates rewards across many epochs without the owner claiming
Cumulative reward exceeds uint96 max (~7.9e28 wei = ~79 billion OLAS at 18 decimals)
High donation volume directed to services using this unit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: uint256 computation result cast to uint96 without bounds check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: N/A - happens during normal donation tracking flow
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When accumulated unclaimed incentives approach uint96 max

operational_questions: What is the realistic maximum accumulation rate for a single unit's reward?
Given the OLAS supply cap (~1B tokens), can reward accumulation practically reach uint96 max?
Should the protocol add overflow checks or safe casting?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum pendingRelativeReward achievable per epoch
Model accumulation over N epochs with maximum donation volumes
Verify that uint96(totalIncentives) truncates without revert in Solidity 0.8

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or create a heavily-used component/agent across many high-donation services
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Do not claim incentives for extended periods to allow accumulation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If totalIncentives exceeds uint96 max during finalization, excess is silently lost
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Claim receives only the truncated amount, permanently losing the overflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control over a popular unit and patience to not claim

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of accumulated rewards for affected units
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Unrecoverable - truncated rewards cannot be restored

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Units with very large pending incentive values approaching uint96 max
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drops in unit reward values after finalization

threats.title: Tokenomics.changeTokenomicsParameters Uses Unsafe uint72/uint32 Casts Enabling Silent Parameter Truncation
threats.hypothesis_description: In changeTokenomicsParameters(), input parameters are cast to smaller types (uint72 for devsPerCapital/codePerDev, uint32 for epochLen) BEFORE being compared against bounds. For example, 'if (uint72(_devsPerCapital) > MIN_PARAM_VALUE)' truncates a uint256 to uint72 before comparison. If the owner (likely a DAO governance contract) passes a value like 2^72 + 1e15, the uint72 cast truncates to 1e15 which passes the MIN_PARAM_VALUE check, and the parameter is set to 1e15 instead of the intended large value. Similar truncation affects epochLen via uint32 cast.
threats.investigation_rationale: When parameters are set via DAO governance proposals, the proposal specifies uint256 values. If the proposal contains a value larger than the target type, it silently truncates without reverting. This could lead to unintended tokenomics parameter configurations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (uint72(_devsPerCapital) > MIN_PARAM_VALUE) { devsPerCapital = uint72(_devsPerCapital); } — cast before comparison allows values > 2^72 to truncate and pass the check

preconditions: Owner passes a uint256 value that exceeds the target type's range
The truncated value still passes the bounds check
Owner is a governance contract executing a proposal with the incorrect value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unsafe downcasting of function parameters before validation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner-only function, but truncation is a code defect
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time changeTokenomicsParameters is called with oversized values

operational_questions: Has any governance proposal ever specified values larger than uint72/uint32 max?
What safeguards exist in the governance proposal creation process?
Should the function revert on overflow instead of silently truncating?
validation_requirements: Call changeTokenomicsParameters with _devsPerCapital = 2^72 + 1e15
Verify devsPerCapital is set to the truncated value without reverting
Confirm the truncated value passes all validation checks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A - this is a code quality defect, not a direct attack vector
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A governance misconfiguration could accidentally set parameters to truncated values
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Truncated parameters affect IDF calculation and epoch timing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Impact depends on which parameter is truncated and by how much
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires governance proposal with oversized parameter

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Tokenomics parameters set to unintended values
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: IDF calculation and epoch timing could be affected
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires another governance proposal to correct

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Parameter values that differ significantly from governance proposal inputs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected parameter values after governance updates

threats.title: StakingFactory Owner Cannot Disable Malicious Staking Instances; Only Original Deployer Has Toggle Authority
threats.hypothesis_description: StakingFactory.setInstanceStatus() restricts instance enable/disable to the original deployer only. The factory owner has no override capability to disable a specific malicious instance. While the factory owner can change the verifier to indirectly deny instances, this is a blunt instrument that affects all instances matching the new criteria. A malicious deployer can repeatedly toggle their instance's status to intermittently DoS claims (via L1 batch revert) while the factory owner has no targeted response capability.
threats.investigation_rationale: The factory is the gatekeeper for emission distribution on L2 (via verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount) and L1 (via EthereumDepositProcessor). If a deployer acts maliciously, the factory owner should have emergency override capability to disable the specific instance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != deployer) { revert OwnerOnly(msg.sender, deployer); } — only deployer can toggle, factory owner has no override path for individual instances

preconditions: Malicious deployer has created a staking instance that initially passed verification
The instance has been voted for as a nominee with non-zero weight
The deployer toggles isEnabled to false

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Factory owner lacks targeted emergency disable for specific instances
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Factory owner can only change verifier globally, not disable specific instances
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Deployer can toggle at any time without restriction

operational_questions: Can the factory owner mitigate via verifier changes without affecting other instances?
Is there a rate limit on setInstanceStatus calls?
Should the factory owner have emergency override capability?
validation_requirements: Verify factory owner cannot call setInstanceStatus for others' instances
Test if verifier can be configured to deny a specific instance without affecting others
Simulate deployer toggling attack pattern

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Get instance added as nominee and voted for
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Toggle isEnabled to false to disrupt claims, then back to true
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat to cause intermittent DoS on emission distribution

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Factory owner lacks fine-grained emergency control
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Intermittent claim failures for affected chains/batches
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires VoteWeighting owner to remove the nominee

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent setInstanceStatus calls toggling between true/false
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Intermittent verifyInstance failures for specific instances
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Instance status changes correlated with claim timing

threats.title: Tokenomics.checkpoint() Lacks Reentrancy Guard Despite External Call to Treasury Before Epoch Counter Increment
threats.hypothesis_description: Tokenomics.checkpoint() makes an external call to ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) before incrementing epochCounter. The contract has a _locked reentrancy variable initialized in the constructor, but checkpoint() neither checks nor modifies it. If the treasury contract is compromised or malicious, it could re-enter checkpoint() during rebalanceTreasury. Since epochCounter hasn't been incremented yet, the reentrancy would process the same epoch data again, potentially corrupting epoch settlement (doubled rewards, inflated effectiveBond, etc.).
threats.investigation_rationale: The _locked variable exists in Tokenomics but is never used in checkpoint(). The external call to treasury occurs late in the function but before the critical epochCounter increment. While treasury is a trusted admin-set contract, the absence of reentrancy protection is a code defect that could have severe consequences if the trust assumption is violated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _locked is set to 1 in initializeTokenomics but never checked in checkpoint(); ITreasury(treasury).rebalanceTreasury(incentives[1]) is called before epochCounter = uint32(eCounter + 1)

preconditions: Treasury contract is compromised or intentionally malicious
Treasury.rebalanceTreasury re-enters Tokenomics.checkpoint()
The re-entrant call passes the delegatecall check, same-block check, and epoch time check

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _locked exists but is not used in checkpoint(); external call before state finalization
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During the rebalanceTreasury external call window

operational_questions: Would a reentrancy in checkpoint actually succeed given the delegatecall and timing checks?
What state corruption would result from double-processing an epoch?
Is the treasury contract immutable or upgradeable?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock treasury that re-enters checkpoint during rebalanceTreasury
Verify whether the second checkpoint call passes all guards
Measure state corruption if reentrancy succeeds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise treasury contract or set a malicious treasury address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Malicious treasury re-enters checkpoint() during rebalanceTreasury callback
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Nested checkpoint processes same epoch again with partially-updated state
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: epochCounter incremented twice or state variables double-counted
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control over treasury contract (admin compromise)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inflation accounting, effectiveBond, and reward distribution corrupted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very difficult - requires understanding exact state corruption

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - requires compromising a trusted contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusual treasury behavior during rebalanceTreasury calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: epochCounter incrementing by more than 1 in a single block
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple checkpoint-related events in a single transaction
